title: Translation and Interpretation Services

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541930 ($2,402,956,156) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541930 ($6,131,619,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Veterinary Assistants and Laboratory Animal Caretakers
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $162,427 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541930); class <5: 2,193 firms, avg revenue/firm $268,027 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $41,946 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 223 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,388,377 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $217,281 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 120 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,092,642 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $483,998 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 95 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,796,705 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $907,184 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $40,892,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,399,598 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $211,163,133 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $33,047,030 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 28
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Written translation is the most AI-displaced task in the economy — MTPE is already the default workflow at documented 30-70%-of-rate pricing. Staff linguists skew toward certified/sworn, high-liability legal/medical work, transcreation and QA of MT output, which persist; live/on-site interpretation is real-time and holds longer. Role share is the guidance hypothesis (~20-30% of W-2 wages) because the provided 5419 role_mix is veterinary-contaminated and unusable.

File prep, TM/termbase management, vendor assignment, automated QA passes and delivery are being automated end-to-end by TMS platforms with AI orchestration; client-side program management, escalation and quality accountability persist. Share per guidance hypothesis (~25-35%).

Appointment scheduling, interpreter matching and telephony routing automate near-fully; interpreting platforms already do this. Residual human layer for escalations and last-minute coverage. Share per guidance hypothesis (~10-15%).
RFP responses and quoting automate; healthcare-system, court and enterprise localization relationships persist and are the firm's real asset. Share per guidance hypothesis (~10-15%).

Capacity planning and margin management automate substantially; compliance ownership (HIPAA, court certification), leadership and client escalation persist. 11-1021 at 6.6% is one of the few plausible signals in the contaminated proxy; ~9% with adjacent management tail.
Invoicing (per-word/per-minute reconciliation), freelancer payables and compliance paperwork automate near-fully with current AI. Share is the remainder of the rebuilt structure (~10-15% guidance).

pi_dist.rationale: Pricing-model deflation is documented, not hypothetical: translation is billed per word with explicit MTPE discount tiers (MTPE priced at 30-70% of the full human per-word rate as the industry-standard rate card), so AI production savings are passed to clients as lower prices by construction — per-word rates have been falling for a decade. Interpreting is per minute/hour, and public payers and hospital procurement re-rate mandated-interpreting contracts at renewal as delivery costs fall, clawing back savings. The operator keeps savings only in the minority of revenue under fixed-fee or mandate-protected contracts. Institutional contracts (statewide court, health-system language access) are firm-owned and transferable, which is why pi_dist is not lower still.

pi_moat.rationale: Real but narrow gates: court interpreter certification requirements, Section 1557/HHS language-access mandates for qualified interpreters in healthcare, USCIS-certified translation, sworn documents, and ISO 17100-type requirements for regulated content genuinely block LLM bypass for those revenue lines; HIPAA and liability requirements add switching friction on institutional contracts. But for ordinary business translation — the bulk of written volume — enterprise clients can and do bypass LSPs with LLM translation plus an in-house reviewer; no license protects the general product, and generic-AI bypass is the central threat to the code.

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct: already ~33% of LSPs had implemented LLMs in workflows in April 2024 (Slator, n=223), with ~20% more testing and only 3% with no plans; super agencies were at 75% and ALC-surveyed companies' LLM-based MT jumped 11%->29% in one year. The historical analog (CAT/TM tools took roughly a decade to become the default) is superseded because LLM adoption is demand-side forced: clients impose MTPE rate cards, so non-adoption means losing bids. Extrapolating the observed ~15-20pp/year implementation growth from ~33% in early 2024, the 50%-of-firms threshold is essentially being crossed now; from today (mid-2026) full material adoption in automatable functions across the small-firm tail puts t50 at ~1.5 years.

current_adoption_pct.source: Slator 2024 Language Industry Market Report — Language AI Edition (survey April 2024, n=223 LSPs)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 'One in three LSPs have implemented large language models into their workflows' (April 2024, 223 companies); 75% of Super Agencies vs just under 30% of Boutiques; ~20% more testing use cases/betas; only 3% report no plans. Material = LLM/MT implemented in production workflows (MTPE, AI QA, workflow orchestration), not mere experimentation. Figure is 2+ years old; current share is plausibly at or above 50%.

historical_analogs.value: Machine translation inside LSP workflows: from niche (~2016, post-NMT) to default — ALC 2024 survey shows LLM-produced MT among MT-using companies jumped from 11% (2023) to 29% (2024), an 18pp single-year gain; CAT/translation-memory tools took roughly a decade (mid-1990s to mid-2000s) to become the professional default. NMT reached majority LSP use in roughly 5-7 years; LLM uptake is tracking faster because it is client-demanded via MTPE rate cards.
historical_analogs.source: Slator coverage of ALC 2024 Industry Survey
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: 'Of 70 companies using MT in their workflows, nearly a third — 29% — said they used LLMs to produce MT', up from 'just 11%' in 2023.

owners_60plus_pct.source: No published owner-age data for US LSPs found (ALC industry survey is member-gated and no owner-age cut is public) — ESTIMATE
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: No public figure. Basis: Census ABS shows >50% of US business owners are 55+ economy-wide, but the LSP owner base skews younger than legacy trades — many firms were founded in the 1990s-2010s localization and remote-interpreting waves, and 2,193 of 2,674 firms are micro businesses with founder-operators across age bands. Central estimate 25% aged 60+.

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: No documented succession shortage found for this industry: sale motivation in current coverage (Slator M&A reports, ALC M&A Corner) is AI uncertainty and consolidation economics, not owner retirement without successors. Flag left false.

active_consolidators.source: Slator M&A coverage + PitchBook/press: Propio (Leonard Green/TT Capital — ULG Sep 2024, Akorbi + ASL Services Oct 2024, CyraCom Jul 2025), Sorenson Communications (Ariel Alternatives — OmniBridge, Hand Talk), GLOBO (VSS Capital — All Access Interpreters), Boostlingo (Mainsail — Interpreter Intelligence, VoiceBoxer), BIG Language Solutions (MSouth Equity), t'works (European PE-backed, US-active tuck-ins)
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Propio alone closed ULG (Sep 2024), Akorbi and ASL Services (Oct 15, 2024) and CyraCom (Jul 1, 2025); Sorenson, GLOBO, Boostlingo and BIG each closed add-ons within the last 24 months. Count = 6 PE-backed platforms with disclosed US add-ons in the window; strategics (TransPerfect, LanguageLine/Teleperformance) excluded from the PE count.

buy_mult.source: Slator M&A coverage of disclosed small-LSP deal multiples
buy_mult.figure_quoted: Disclosed small-deal prints: HeterMedia/Into23 at '6.3x EBITDA'; Stoquart/Version Internationale at 'around 5x adjusted EBITDA' (adjusted = post owner-comp normalization per rule 3). Median of recent disclosed boutique prints ~5.5x; Slator notes most small-deal prices go undisclosed.

exit_mult.source: Teleperformance acquisition of LanguageLine Solutions (2016) — the benchmark scaled-platform print in this code; corroborating current activity: Propio/CyraCom (2025, undisclosed)
exit_mult.figure_quoted: LanguageLine: 'revenue of US$388 million and adjusted EBITDA of US$147 million in 2015', acquired for '$1.5 billion' — ~10.2x adjusted EBITDA. Interpreting-heavy platforms remain the exit route (Propio recapped by Leonard Green 2023; CyraCom bought 2025 at undisclosed multiple). Dated print; current AI overhang argues exits at or below this level for translation-heavy platforms.

pricing_structure.value: Translation: per-word rate cards with explicit MTPE discount tiers (MTPE at 30-70% of full human per-word rate; light post-editing ~40-50%, full post-editing ~50-70%) — the dominant model for written work; some fixed-fee project and rare retainer arrangements. Interpreting: per-minute (OPI/VRI) and per-hour/per-encounter (on-site, court) rates, often under institutional contracts with hospitals, courts and insurers, re-rated at renewal. Effectively all revenue is per-unit priced; almost none is value-priced.
pricing_structure.source: Weglot MTPE pricing guide; Nimdzi MTPE pricing analysis
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: 'MTPE rates usually fall between 30% and 70% of a standard human translation rate'; light post-editing '40 to 50 percent of the full per-word rate', full post-editing '50 to 70 percent'.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.2032 implies ~20.3pp of revenue freed at full adoption — far above the 3pp floor — and A = 6.67 is high. High uplift with fast adoption is internally consistent and matches the observed reality: adoption is client-forced through MTPE rate cards. Note the capture caveat: C = 1.35 says most of that 20pp is competed away to clients rather than kept.

terminal_value.justification: This is the canonical melter: LLMs produce the product itself — translated text — at near-zero marginal cost. The substitutable bulk (ordinary business translation, most written work) is already collapsing into MT-with-review at falling per-word rates, and at mature AI much of it becomes client-side self-service. What survives as paid demand is the mandate-protected floor: statutorily required live interpretation (Section 1557 healthcare encounters, court certification, ADA/deaf services — Sorenson/LanguageLine territory), certification-gated document translation (USCIS, sworn/legal), and liability-bearing regulated content. For a typical US firm in this code, that floor is plausibly on the order of a third to a half of revenue (interpreting-heavy firms more, translation agencies less), and even the interpreting floor faces AI speech translation on remote modalities plus payer re-rating. Demand for the product category as sold by these firms is shrinking structurally: MELTING, with a mandate-protected interpreting residue that is merely PRESSURED.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed. V was built from the labor_share dataset input and a rebuilt LSP role structure with fractions grounded in task-level AI capability (MTPE workflows, TMS automation, scheduling platforms) — no deal evidence used. A was derived from the Slator and ALC adoption surveys and MT/CAT diffusion analogs. Consolidation activity (Propio, Sorenson, GLOBO, Boostlingo, BIG) informed only active_consolidators (B) and the multiple inputs (M).
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing is effectively 100% per-unit: per word (translation) and per minute/hour/encounter (interpreting). Deflation is documented, not hypothetical: MTPE discount tiers price AI work at 30-70% of the human rate, i.e., the rate card itself passes AI savings to clients; interpreting contracts are re-rated downward at renewal by hospital procurement and public payers. Only fixed-fee and mandate-protected revenue retains savings. This is exactly why pi_dist = 0.30 (low) despite institutional contract ownership, and why pi_moat = 0.45 covers only the certification/liability-gated lines; C = 1.35 is the record's expression of near-total pass-through.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges 4.0-10.0; S ranges ~3.50-4.20. Adoption is demonstrably fast, but 'material adoption by 50% of firms' among the 2,193 micro-firms could lag the headline survey base, which skews toward larger LSPs.

V ranges ~6.35-9.69; S ranges ~3.69-4.01. The entire role structure is a hypothesis because the provided 5419 role_mix is veterinary-contaminated; no W-2 wage-bill decomposition for actual LSPs is published.

M ranges ~1.08 (buy 6.3, exit 8) to ~5.6 (buy 5.0, exit 12); S ranges ~3.08-4.28. The exit anchor is a 2016 print (LanguageLine 10.2x); current AI overhang may have compressed exits for translation-heavy platforms well below it — this is the biggest downside risk to M.
OW 0.143-0.714; B ranges ~2.02-4.51; S ranges ~3.47-4.08. Pure estimate — no public owner-age data for LSPs.

reviewer_note: Heterogeneous: the code blends written translation (the melting half) and interpreting (mandate-protected, growing, physically/real-time delivered); blended firm-level economics mask opposite terminal trajectories, and sign-language/TRS (Sorenson), captioning (512191 boundary) and TMS software (511210/513210) straddle the boundary. Dataset conflicts, used as given per rule 6: (1) the provided 4-digit 5419 role_mix is veterinary-dominated (veterinarians 17.1% wage share; 27-3091 Interpreters and Translators only 2.1%) and unusable as an LSP proxy — per the prompt's own guidance the V breakdown was rebuilt from the hypothesized LSP W-2 structure, so ai_replaceable_share is LOW quality; (2) labor_share 0.4178 covers only the W-2 wage bill — most production linguists are 1099 freelancers sitting in COGS (LSP gross margins run 40-50% over freelance spend), so the true language-labor content AI attacks is materially larger than V_raw implies and V = 8.13 is, if anything, understated for economic exposure while overstated as owner-captured opportunity (C = 1.35 carries that correction); (3) n_band = 28 makes the roll-up universe extremely thin — the 100-499 employee class only — and the 15 largest firms are already consolidator-owned; the 95-firm 20-99 class sits just below the $1M EBITDA line at Damodaran's 15.65% margin, so n_band is sensitive to the margin assumption. Score tension to flag honestly: S = 3.87 looks mid-pack, but it is the product of a high-V/high-A cost-savings engine crossed with the worst capture (C = 1.35) and a MELTING terminal class — the geometric mean hides that the value largely does not accrue to the operator; a reviewer should read this record as 'fast, valuable AI, mostly competed away, thin target universe, product melting'. The exit_mult is a 2016 print used for lack of newer disclosed platform multiples; treat M = 3.42 as optimistic if translation-heavy exits have re-rated under AI overhang. succession bonus not applied (no documented shortage). No formula overrides; all scores are mechanical outputs of the recorded inputs.
